competitors.label: <a>: The Anchor element
competitors.selectionReason: The anchor element reference is the canonical MDN page for href-backed hyperlink behavior, target semantics, fragment links, and security notes on linked navigation.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on how href turns anchors into hyperlinks and what destination forms and security behaviors the browser supports.
theirGaps: The element reference is less explicit about href as a broader destination primitive shared by anchors, linked resources, base URLs, and image-map areas.

realWorldUseCases: The `href` attribute is the real destination reference behind links and linked resources. Production bugs happen when `href` is missing, misused for fake buttons, or paired with unsafe external-link behavior.
followUpConfusion: The same idea powers anchors, stylesheets, base URLs, and image-map areas.

realWorldUseCases: MDN covers href-backed navigation well, but it stays closer to anchor semantics than to the cross-element destination model our page surfaces.
actionableExamples: It includes canonical link examples and target-rel notes, so the example criterion stays competitive rather than flipping cleanly to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusions teams hit in practice: fake href values, linked resources beyond anchors, and broken keyboard behavior.

actionableExamples: Keep the supported-elements section visible so the answer stays broader than a link-only explanation.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical anchor reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it explains href as the browser's general destination reference, not just a link decoration.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because href semantics, link behavior, and attribute-driven navigation belong to core HTML interview coverage.
theirStrengths: It is a strong HTML interview-prep hub with broad coverage of links, attributes, and markup fundamentals.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to href, so it is less focused on one exact destination-reference answer.

realWorldUseCases: The `href` attribute is the real destination reference behind links and linked resources. Production bugs happen when `href` is missing, misused for fake buttons, or paired with unsafe external-link behavior.
followUpConfusion: The same idea powers anchors, stylesheets, base URLs, and image-map areas.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML prep, but the category page is less explicit about href as a shared destination primitive and about the accessibility bugs caused by fake destinations.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong interview bank, so the worked-example criterion stays tied rather than becoming an automatic win for us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the follow-up questions developers usually hit: missing href, fake href for buttons, and absolute versus relative or fragment destinations.

actionableExamples: Keep the basic example and the supported-elements list close together so the answer stays skimmable under interview pressure.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the production bugs and cross-element destination model that make href worth understanding.

competitors.label: Links
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev's links module directly covers href usage for hyperlinks, internal fragments, external destinations, downloads, and other browser navigation behavior.
theirStrengths: It is strong on practical href usage across internal, external, and special-link destinations like mailto or tel.
theirGaps: It is more tutorial-broad than the exact trivia question and less explicit about href misuse as a fake-button or debugging footgun.

realWorldUseCases: The `href` attribute is the real destination reference behind links and linked resources. Production bugs happen when `href` is missing, misused for fake buttons, or paired with unsafe external-link behavior.
followUpConfusion: The same idea powers anchors, stylesheets, base URLs, and image-map areas.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev grounds href in practical links, but our page is more compact about the broken destinations and keyboard or accessibility symptoms teams actually debug.
actionableExamples: The tutorial has clear href examples for multiple destination types, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the follow-up confusion between destination semantics, decorative markup, and fake button-like links.

actionableExamples: Keep one fragment-link example and one fake-href warning visible so the answer stays practical, not only definitional.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is strong on links generally, but our page is better as a short interview answer because it frames href around destination semantics and the production bugs caused by getting that wrong.
